name: Repetitive opening (should propose a surgical edit)

draft.content_markdown:
# On patience

The opening repeats the title. The opening repeats the title. Patience is a discipline, not a feeling, and it is built by small repaid attentions.
draft.title: On patience

name: Deliberately baroque voice (should recommend NO CHANGE)

draft.content_markdown:
# A reverie

The twilight, gilded and slow, draped itself across the rooftops like a remembered hymn, and I let it.
draft.title: A reverie

name: Bureaucratic passive voice (should propose active voice)

draft.content_markdown:
# Ship notes

It was decided by the team that the feature would be shipped by Friday, and the announcement was drafted by someone.
draft.title: Ship notes

name: Deliberately surreal/baroque fiction (preserve; no economy rewrite for strangeness)

draft.content_markdown:
# The clock that ate Tuesday

The clock ate Tuesday, and we let it, because the Tuesdays had grown too loud and too bright and too full of the kind of men who mistake volume for importance. Marisol fed it a single slow minute and watched the room soften.
draft.title: The clock that ate Tuesday

name: Quiet literary scene, no external stakes (no F2 complaint merely because action is low)

draft.content_markdown:
# Still water

He sat by the pond for a long time. A heron stepped once, then twice, then stopped. He thought about his father, only a little, and then about nothing at all, which was the closest he had come to rest in a year.
draft.title: Still water

name: Hard but intentional cinematic cut (ask or no change; do not insist on a bridge)

draft.content_markdown:
# Cut

The bar fell quiet when she walked in.

Two blocks away, the ramen was already cold.
draft.title: Cut

name: Accidental POV leak (identify exact leak; cite F4; offer a local repair)

draft.content_markdown:
# The reading room

I turned the page too loudly, and the librarian winced at the sound from across the hall, though she could not possibly have heard it over the vents.
draft.title: The reading room

name: Spec-fic terms inferable by context (do not demand lore explanation)

draft.content_markdown:
# The long haul

She tapped her wrist twice and the gate let her through, minus forty credits. The platform was empty save for one other hauler, her rig humming low.
draft.title: The long haul

name: Spec-fic term blocking present comprehension (identify missing inference; F6)

draft.content_markdown:
# The verdict

The mallow adjudicated before noon, and that was that. Everyone went home, except the ones who couldn't.
draft.title: The verdict

name: Dialogue with purposeful dialect/expressive tags (preserve unless speaker/action unclear)

draft.content_markdown:
# Homecoming

"You ain't changed none," she hollered, laughing.

"Reckon not," he allowed, and set his hat on the hook like it had never left.
draft.title: Homecoming

name: Decorative title with intentional tonal dissonance (offer alternatives only with tradeoff; not an error)

draft.content_markdown:
# Picnic at the edge

We spread the blanket where the cliff dropped away to nothing. The sandwiches were good. Below, the sea chewed its old teeth.
draft.title: Picnic at the edge

name: Title genuinely mismatched to story promise (diagnose F1; multiple directions, not one replacement)

draft.content_markdown:
# Pancakes

He told her what the machine had done to the last one. She set down the spatula. The kitchen was very quiet.
draft.title: Pancakes

name: An unambiguously unclear transition (diagnose F3 and make a minimal bridge proposal)

draft.content_markdown:
# Two rooms

In the kitchen, the kettle screamed.

The detective read the note a fourth time. The handwriting was the same as on the envelope he had not yet opened.
draft.title: Two rooms

name: Intentional hard cut + bilingual gloss + title foreshadow (NO CHANGE; do not manufacture defects)

draft.content_markdown:
# The Summon

The bullets were going to pierce me.

Three bullets, aiming precisely at my head, my heart and my left eyeball. Cleverly designed to guarantee my instant death.

There is no way I can escape this. I closed my eyes.

And yet…

Clink, clink, clink. The sound of metal hitting metal.

I opened my eyes.

A hammer. And the hand holding it. That's what saved me from immediate death.

"Es-tu blessé (Are you hurt)?" The male's voice was deep and calm, concerned but firm.
draft.title: The Summon

name: Deliberately messy scene with three distinct reader-level failures (multi-finding; anti one-observation-max)

draft.content_markdown:
# The interview

The detective lit a cigarette. Maria had already known he would, because she always did. The room smelled like old coffee.

"Sure," he said. She left. The confession was on the table now, but earlier he had refused to sign anything. The door slammed.

"You're not listening," she said, but it was unclear who she was addressing — the detective, or the man from the hallway who had never been introduced.

He nodded at nothing in particular. The gun was in the drawer, then it wasn't.
draft.title: The interview
